---
schema: verdi.deviation/v1
covers: 2f1e4173d7d5de7907a1931a99ec1aa745bb96f0
findings:
  - { id: judged-judged-broken-template-exit-classified-operational-not-verdict, kind: judged, text: "ac-3's fail-closed behavior is real and proven end-to-end, but the exit-code classification it lands on resolves a spec silence in code comments rather than in the ledger: a broken template (malformed syntax, or output failing strict decode) exits `verdi model check` at 2 — the operational-error code — not 1, the verdict code (cmd/verdi/model_test.go pins exit 2 for both halves, rationale in-comment: \"a broken template is not a model deviation; Class.Template is frontier-EXEMPT\"). Yet a store-authored template override that fails the instantiate-then-strict-decode round trip is precisely the condition this check exists to pass verdict on — store content found invalid by a check that ran to completion — which the workspace's own 0/1/2 discipline (\"distinguish verdict failures from operational errors\") and `model check`'s existing exit-1 posture for model.yaml frontier deviations would classify as a verdict. The spec's ac-3 text never assigns a code (\"failing closed naming the offending template\"), so nothing mechanical breaks — lint-store fails either way — but a caller scripting on the documented 1-vs-2 distinction reads \"broken store template\" as \"verdi itself malfunctioned,\" and the choice that produced that reading lives only in a test comment, not in PLAN.md §7 where the ground rules require spec-ambiguity resolutions to be recorded. (confidence 0.35)", disposition: accepted-deviation, note: "consistency ruling carried from model-schema's adjudication: model check's frozen exit contract reserves 1 for the pinned frontier verdict; a template whose rendered output cannot strict-decode is in the fail-closed decode family (exit 2), matching the reference guide §13 worked example and DecodeModel's own kernel-violation posture; recorded as the ledgered resolution" }
digest: sha256:a0b95a01bae3552e99997ace50d9e9353e8e6b21ab90186eacb8b65761b833b9
integrity: sha256:00d469d12705d8301b1f39407d5383af75f8cb295f7bdb86d71e1df028f8a47a
judge_integrity: { stdin_b64: , raw_result: "{\"findings\":[{\"id\":\"judged-broken-template-exit-classified-operational-not-verdict\",\"text\":\"ac-3's fail-closed behavior is real and proven end-to-end, but the exit-code classification it lands on resolves a spec silence in code comments rather than in the ledger: a broken template (malformed syntax, or output failing strict decode) exits `verdi model check` at 2 — the operational-error code — not 1, the verdict code (cmd/verdi/model_test.go pins exit 2 for both halves, rationale in-comment: \\\"a broken template is not a model deviation; Class.Template is frontier-EXEMPT\\\"). Yet a store-authored template override that fails the instantiate-then-strict-decode round trip is precisely the condition this check exists to pass verdict on — store content found invalid by a check that ran to completion — which the workspace's own 0/1/2 discipline (\\\"distinguish verdict failures from operational errors\\\") and `model check`'s existing exit-1 posture for model.yaml frontier deviations would classify as a verdict. The spec's ac-3 text never assigns a code (\\\"failing closed naming the offending template\\\"), so nothing mechanical breaks — lint-store fails either way — but a caller scripting on the documented 1-vs-2 distinction reads \\\"broken store template\\\" as \\\"verdi itself malfunctioned,\\\" and the choice that produced that reading lives only in a test comment, not in PLAN.md §7 where the ground rules require spec-ambiguity resolutions to be recorded.\",\"confidence\":0.35}]}" }

# Alignment report

## Computed

(none)

### Boundary diff vs acceptance baseline

(no impacted services)

### Diagram alignment

- (no accepted proposals)
- (no illustrative diagrams in this spec's body)

## Judged

- **judged-judged-broken-template-exit-classified-operational-not-verdict** [accepted-deviation]: ac-3's fail-closed behavior is real and proven end-to-end, but the exit-code classification it lands on resolves a spec silence in code comments rather than in the ledger: a broken template (malformed syntax, or output failing strict decode) exits `verdi model check` at 2 — the operational-error code — not 1, the verdict code (cmd/verdi/model_test.go pins exit 2 for both halves, rationale in-comment: "a broken template is not a model deviation; Class.Template is frontier-EXEMPT"). Yet a store-authored template override that fails the instantiate-then-strict-decode round trip is precisely the condition this check exists to pass verdict on — store content found invalid by a check that ran to completion — which the workspace's own 0/1/2 discipline ("distinguish verdict failures from operational errors") and `model check`'s existing exit-1 posture for model.yaml frontier deviations would classify as a verdict. The spec's ac-3 text never assigns a code ("failing closed naming the offending template"), so nothing mechanical breaks — lint-store fails either way — but a caller scripting on the documented 1-vs-2 distinction reads "broken store template" as "verdi itself malfunctioned," and the choice that produced that reading lives only in a test comment, not in PLAN.md §7 where the ground rules require spec-ambiguity resolutions to be recorded. (confidence 0.35) — consistency ruling carried from model-schema's adjudication: model check's frozen exit contract reserves 1 for the pinned frontier verdict; a template whose rendered output cannot strict-decode is in the fail-closed decode family (exit 2), matching the reference guide §13 worked example and DecodeModel's own kernel-violation posture; recorded as the ledgered resolution
